content: When you first encounter the pattern of passing a closure as an output parameter, your brain likely screams 'what if I just return the value?' and immediately tries to map this to how C or Python handle functions. That instinct is exactly where the mental model breaks down because Rust's ownership system forces us to rethink what it means to produce data versus consuming data. Think of a function not as a factory that builds an object you hold onto, but as a pipe that processes input and directs output elsewhere. When we pass a closure into a function like `process_lines`, we aren't just passing a function; we are providing the destination for the data stream itself. This is the idiomatic Rust way to handle streaming because it decouples the producer logic from the consumer logic, allowing the producer to focus solely on generating values while the closure handles buffering, logging, or sending them over a network socket without ever needing to know about the internal state of the generator. Imagine you have an iterator that yields millions of rows from a massive CSV file; returning all those rows into memory would crash your application due to stack overflow or heap exhaustion, but by accepting a `&mut dyn Sink<Bytes>` closure, the function streams each row directly to the sink as it is produced. This approach respects Rust's zero-cost abstractions because the compiler generates code that avoids any intermediate allocation for the return value; instead, the data flows through the memory of the closure's buffer or network connection in real-time. You might wonder why we don't just use a `Vec` and then `extend` it later, which seems simpler on paper. That approach forces the entire dataset to exist simultaneously in memory before any processing can occur on it, defeating the purpose of streaming entirely. The closure pattern allows us to process records one at a time, keeping our peak memory usage constant regardless of how large the input stream grows. Furthermore, this design scales beautifully when you need to chain operations; instead of returning a `Vec<T>` and then calling `.into_iter()` on it downstream, you can pass the same sink closure through multiple processing stages like filtering or aggregation without needing intermediate containers that hold the full dataset in flight. The key mental shift here is viewing functions as stateless processors that take an input stream and an output channel, rather than factories that produce a result object which then gets consumed elsewhere. This also ties into Rust's trait system where traits like `Iterator` and `Sink` provide the abstractions needed to write generic code that works with any type of data source or sink, whether it's a file handle, a socket, or an in-memory buffer. If you're coming from languages like JavaScript or Python where functions often return objects that are then passed around by reference, Rust forces us to explicitly model the flow direction through these parameters. This explicitness prevents subtle bugs related to premature data collection or race conditions when multiple threads try to write to shared buffers because each thread has its own closure instance managing its own stream destination. In practice, this looks like a function signature that takes an `&mut dyn Sink` as an argument, allowing the function body to call `.sink()` on every yielded item directly rather than accumulating them in a local variable. The beauty of this pattern is that it makes the intent of your code immediately clear to any reader scanning the source files, showing exactly where data goes without requiring them to mentally construct intermediate data structures. It also aligns perfectly with Rust's philosophy of explicit memory management and performance by eliminating unnecessary heap allocations for temporary collections, ensuring your application can handle truly massive datasets that would otherwise cause a panic in traditional imperative languages. So when designing the next big data pipeline or real-time analytics system you are building, remember to ask yourself: am I trying to collect everything first, or am I directing the flow as it happens? The answer almost always points toward passing a sink closure instead of returning values.
